claim: Solstice is a leader-based Byzantine fault tolerant consensus protocol designed for permissioned networks of a few hundred to a few thousand validators.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice is a leader-based Byzantine fault tolerant consensus protocol designed for permissioned networks of a few hundred to a few thousand validators.

claim: A block becomes committed once votes from more than two-thirds of the committee's total stake have been collected and aggregated into a quorum certificate.

supporting_evidence.quote: A block becomes committed once votes from more than two-thirds of the committee's total stake have been collected and aggregated into a quorum certificate.

claim: When a leader fails to produce a proposal within its allotted window, the committee moves to a view change and rotates to the next leader in sequence.

supporting_evidence.quote: When a leader fails to produce a proposal within its allotted window, the committee moves to a view change and rotates to the next leader in sequence.

claim: Repeated view changes without progress are rare in practice, but the protocol tolerates an arbitrary number of them without compromising safety.

supporting_evidence.quote: Repeated view changes without progress are rare in practice, but the protocol tolerates an arbitrary number of them without compromising safety.

claim: Each view change roughly doubles the timeout window for the next round, a backoff strategy borrowed from earlier partially synchronous protocols.

supporting_evidence.quote: Each view change roughly doubles the timeout window for the next round, a backoff strategy borrowed from earlier partially synchronous protocols.

claim: A quorum certificate is an aggregated signature proving that a threshold fraction of the committee's stake voted for a specific proposal.

supporting_evidence.quote: A quorum certificate is an aggregated signature proving that a threshold fraction of the committee's stake voted for a specific proposal.

claim: Solstice uses a threshold BLS signature scheme so that a certificate remains a single, constant-size object regardless of committee size.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice uses a threshold BLS signature scheme so that a certificate remains a single, constant-size object regardless of committee size.

claim: Under favorable network conditions, Solstice achieves what the literature calls optimistic responsiveness: it commits as fast as the actual network allows rather than waiting for a fixed timeout.

supporting_evidence.quote: Under favorable network conditions, Solstice achieves what the literature calls optimistic responsiveness: it commits as fast as the actual network allows rather than waiting for a fixed timeout.

claim: The pipelining depth is configurable, and deployments with high inter-validator latency typically run three or four rounds in flight simultaneously.

supporting_evidence.quote: The pipelining depth is configurable, and deployments with high inter-validator latency typically run three or four rounds in flight simultaneously.

claim: Safety, meaning no two honest validators ever commit conflicting blocks at the same height, holds unconditionally, even during arbitrarily long asynchronous periods.

supporting_evidence.quote: Safety, meaning no two honest validators ever commit conflicting blocks at the same height, holds unconditionally, even during arbitrarily long asynchronous periods.

claim: Liveness, meaning the committee eventually commits new blocks, is only guaranteed once the network stabilizes into the bounded-delay regime the protocol assumes.

supporting_evidence.quote: Liveness, meaning the committee eventually commits new blocks, is only guaranteed once the network stabilizes into the bounded-delay regime the protocol assumes.

claim: Earlier protocols in this family required each validator to collect and verify individual signatures from every other committee member, an approach that scales quadratically with committee size.

supporting_evidence.quote: Earlier protocols in this family required each validator to collect and verify individual signatures from every other committee member, an approach that scales quadratically with committee size.

claim: Solstice's threshold-signature aggregation instead scales linearly, which is what makes committees of several thousand validators practical rather than merely theoretical.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice's threshold-signature aggregation instead scales linearly, which is what makes committees of several thousand validators practical rather than merely theoretical.

claim: Operators running a Solstice validator are advised to co-locate their signing key inside a hardware security module rather than storing it on the validator's general-purpose disk.

supporting_evidence.quote: Operators running a Solstice validator are advised to co-locate their signing key inside a hardware security module rather than storing it on the validator's general-purpose disk.

claim: Validators typically run behind a sentry-node architecture, where a public-facing relay shields the actual signing validator's network address from the open internet.

supporting_evidence.quote: Validators typically run behind a sentry-node architecture, where a public-facing relay shields the actual signing validator's network address from the open internet.

claim: Solstice 1.0 shipped in June 2018 with a fixed committee size of 100 validators and no support for dynamic membership changes.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice 1.0 shipped in June 2018 with a fixed committee size of 100 validators and no support for dynamic membership changes.

claim: Solstice 2.0 arrived in September 2020, replacing per-validator signature verification with the threshold BLS aggregation scheme now used in production.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice 2.0 arrived in September 2020, replacing per-validator signature verification with the threshold BLS aggregation scheme now used in production.

claim: Solstice 3.0, released in January 2023, introduced the exponential view-change backoff and reduced median block time from 2.4 seconds to 1.1 seconds under the reference benchmark.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice 3.0, released in January 2023, introduced the exponential view-change backoff and reduced median block time from 2.4 seconds to 1.1 seconds under the reference benchmark.

claim: As of the foundation's fourth-quarter 2023 report, the largest public Solstice deployment ran 2,847 active validators across 41 countries.

supporting_evidence.quote: As of the foundation's fourth-quarter 2023 report, the largest public Solstice deployment ran 2,847 active validators across 41 countries.

claim: That deployment processed a daily average of 6.2 million transactions, with a peak single-day count of 19.4 million during a widely publicized token migration.

supporting_evidence.quote: That deployment processed a daily average of 6.2 million transactions, with a peak single-day count of 19.4 million during a widely publicized token migration.

claim: Median finality latency across that period was 1.3 seconds, with the ninety-ninth percentile reaching 4.8 seconds during a brief network partition in August 2023.

supporting_evidence.quote: Median finality latency across that period was 1.3 seconds, with the ninety-ninth percentile reaching 4.8 seconds during a brief network partition in August 2023.

claim: The Meridian Consensus Benchmark, known as MCB, is a standardized workload the wider consensus research community uses to compare leader-based protocols under identical network conditions.

supporting_evidence.quote: The Meridian Consensus Benchmark, known as MCB, is a standardized workload the wider consensus research community uses to compare leader-based protocols under identical network conditions.

claim: MCB version 2, released in 2020, extended the original suite with a wide-area network profile modeling validators spread across five continents.

supporting_evidence.quote: MCB version 2, released in 2020, extended the original suite with a wide-area network profile modeling validators spread across five continents.

claim: Under the MCB v2 wide-area profile, Solstice 2.0 sustained 3,400 transactions per second at a median latency of 1.6 seconds.

supporting_evidence.quote: Under the MCB v2 wide-area profile, Solstice 2.0 sustained 3,400 transactions per second at a median latency of 1.6 seconds.

claim: One frequently cited real-world test of wide-area consensus latency comes from the Neptune-9 buoy array, an oceanographic sensor network whose control plane briefly ran an experimental Solstice deployment.

supporting_evidence.quote: One frequently cited real-world test of wide-area consensus latency comes from the Neptune-9 buoy array, an oceanographic sensor network whose control plane briefly ran an experimental Solstice deployment.

claim: The Neptune-9 buoy array was deployed in 2016 across a scattered set of moorings connected only by intermittent satellite uplinks.

supporting_evidence.quote: The Neptune-9 buoy array was deployed in 2016 across a scattered set of moorings connected only by intermittent satellite uplinks.

claim: The experiment nonetheless confirmed that Solstice's safety guarantee held even when liveness degraded sharply under the buoy array's extreme and variable latency.

supporting_evidence.quote: The experiment nonetheless confirmed that Solstice's safety guarantee held even when liveness degraded sharply under the buoy array's extreme and variable latency.

claim: The relationship between committee size and Byzantine fault tolerance follows the standard n >= 3f + 1 bound used throughout the partially synchronous consensus literature.

supporting_evidence.quote: The relationship between committee size and Byzantine fault tolerance follows the standard n >= 3f + 1 bound used throughout the partially synchronous consensus literature.

claim: Operators sizing a new committee typically pick n well above the minimum bound, both to tolerate unplanned validator downtime and to leave room for future growth without a disruptive resize.

supporting_evidence.quote: Operators sizing a new committee typically pick n well above the minimum bound, both to tolerate unplanned validator downtime and to leave room for future growth without a disruptive resize.

claim: Solstice's throughput depends heavily on pipelining depth and committee size, both of which operators can tune independently of the protocol's core logic.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice's throughput depends heavily on pipelining depth and committee size, both of which operators can tune independently of the protocol's core logic.

claim: These figures come from the reference benchmark harness shipped alongside the Solstice 3.0 release, run on identical cloud instances across all five rows.

supporting_evidence.quote: These figures come from the reference benchmark harness shipped alongside the Solstice 3.0 release, run on identical cloud instances across all five rows.

claim: Joining the active Solstice committee requires completing a fixed onboarding sequence before a new validator's votes are counted toward any quorum.

supporting_evidence.quote: Joining the active Solstice committee requires completing a fixed onboarding sequence before a new validator's votes are counted toward any quorum.

claim: Skipping the activation delay is not possible by design; the committee's membership contract enforces it independently of the validator's own software.

supporting_evidence.quote: Skipping the activation delay is not possible by design; the committee's membership contract enforces it independently of the validator's own software.

claim: Operators troubleshooting a misbehaving validator typically start from the following checklist of well-documented failure modes.

supporting_evidence.quote: Operators troubleshooting a misbehaving validator typically start from the following checklist of well-documented failure modes.

claim: Most reported incidents trace back to the first two items, both of which are detectable from the validator's own local logs well before they affect the wider committee.

supporting_evidence.quote: Most reported incidents trace back to the first two items, both of which are detectable from the validator's own local logs well before they affect the wider committee.

claim: Okubo and Fujita's リーダー交代型合意プロトコルの安全性 (2016) proves the safety argument that Solstice's specification cites directly in its formal appendix.

supporting_evidence.quote: Okubo and Fujita's リーダー交代型合意プロトコルの安全性 (2016) proves the safety argument that Solstice's specification cites directly in its formal appendix.

claim: Volkov's Согласованность при частичной синхронности (2017) independently derives the same quorum-intersection bound using a different proof technique based on adversary scheduling.

supporting_evidence.quote: Volkov's Согласованность при частичной синхронности (2017) independently derives the same quorum-intersection bound using a different proof technique based on adversary scheduling.

claim: Solstice's threshold signature scheme follows parameter recommendations published by a working group whose proceedings appear primarily in French: Securite des schemas de signature a seuil, or 'Security of threshold signature schemes' (2019), rendered there as Sécurité des schémas de signature à seuil.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice's threshold signature scheme follows parameter recommendations published by a working group whose proceedings appear primarily in French: Securite des schemas de signature a seuil, or 'Security of threshold signature schemes' (2019), rendered there as Sécurité des schémas de signature à seuil.

claim: The symbol λ denotes the security parameter throughout both reports, matching the convention Solstice's own specification adopts for consistency.

supporting_evidence.quote: The symbol λ denotes the security parameter throughout both reports, matching the convention Solstice's own specification adopts for consistency.

claim: Let n be the total committee stake and f the maximum Byzantine stake, with the standard assumption n >= 3f + 1.

supporting_evidence.quote: Let n be the total committee stake and f the maximum Byzantine stake, with the standard assumption n >= 3f + 1.

claim: A quorum is any set of validators whose combined stake exceeds 2n/3; safety requires that any two quorums share at least one honest member.

supporting_evidence.quote: A quorum is any set of validators whose combined stake exceeds 2n/3; safety requires that any two quorums share at least one honest member.

claim: If each leader independently has probability p of being faulty or unreachable, the expected number of failed leaders before a functioning one is chosen is p/(1-p).

supporting_evidence.quote: If each leader independently has probability p of being faulty or unreachable, the expected number of failed leaders before a functioning one is chosen is p/(1-p).

claim: Substituting the standard bound f/n < 1/3 gives an expected view-change count below 1/2, meaning most rounds succeed on the very first leader.

supporting_evidence.quote: Substituting the standard bound f/n < 1/3 gives an expected view-change count below 1/2, meaning most rounds succeed on the very first leader.

claim: The breakthrough came from adopting a threshold BLS signature scheme, which had been used in smaller academic prototypes but never deployed at the committee sizes Solstice targeted.

supporting_evidence.quote: The breakthrough came from adopting a threshold BLS signature scheme, which had been used in smaller academic prototypes but never deployed at the committee sizes Solstice targeted.

claim: The first public specification, published in 2018 alongside the 1.0 release, was deliberately conservative: fixed committee size, no dynamic membership, and a simple linear leader rotation with no exponential backoff.

supporting_evidence.quote: The first public specification, published in 2018 alongside the 1.0 release, was deliberately conservative: fixed committee size, no dynamic membership, and a simple linear leader rotation with no exponential backoff.

claim: Looking back, the maintainers point to the threshold-signature decision as the single change that mattered most, and to the multi-year gap before adaptive timeouts as the clearest example of a known problem left unfixed for longer than it should have been.

supporting_evidence.quote: Looking back, the maintainers point to the threshold-signature decision as the single change that mattered most, and to the multi-year gap before adaptive timeouts as the clearest example of a known problem left unfixed for longer than it should have been.

claim: On August 14th, 2023, a submarine cable fault in the Java Sea severed the primary network path connecting eleven Solstice validators located across three Southeast Asian data centers from the rest of the committee.

supporting_evidence.quote: On August 14th, 2023, a submarine cable fault in the Java Sea severed the primary network path connecting eleven Solstice validators located across three Southeast Asian data centers from the rest of the committee.

claim: Connectivity was restored six hours later when the regional network operator rerouted traffic over a secondary path, at which point the eleven validators resynchronized their certificate chains and resumed voting within the next full round.

supporting_evidence.quote: Connectivity was restored six hours later when the regional network operator rerouted traffic over a secondary path, at which point the eleven validators resynchronized their certificate chains and resumed voting within the next full round.

claim: The postmortem's central conclusion was that the protocol's safety and liveness guarantees performed exactly as the specification promised: no incorrect block was ever committed, and liveness degraded gracefully in proportion to the fraction of the committee actually affected, rather than collapsing entirely.

supporting_evidence.quote: The postmortem's central conclusion was that the protocol's safety and liveness guarantees performed exactly as the specification promised: no incorrect block was ever committed, and liveness degraded gracefully in proportion to the fraction of the committee actually affected, rather than collapsing entirely.

claim: This section recaps committee recovery behavior for readers skimming the specification's operational appendix rather than the full protocol description.

supporting_evidence.quote: This section recaps committee recovery behavior for readers skimming the specification's operational appendix rather than the full protocol description.

claim: Recovery time in practice depends heavily on how quickly the new leader's proposal reaches the rest of the committee, which is primarily a function of network topology rather than protocol logic.

supporting_evidence.quote: Recovery time in practice depends heavily on how quickly the new leader's proposal reaches the rest of the committee, which is primarily a function of network topology rather than protocol logic.

claim: This recap consolidates the version-history highlights most frequently requested by teams evaluating Solstice for a new deployment.

supporting_evidence.quote: This recap consolidates the version-history highlights most frequently requested by teams evaluating Solstice for a new deployment.

claim: The aggregation scheme introduced in that release remains unchanged through the current 3.0 release, aside from the parameter updates described in the international standards references.

supporting_evidence.quote: The aggregation scheme introduced in that release remains unchanged through the current 3.0 release, aside from the parameter updates described in the international standards references.

claim: A committee of 100 validators tolerates at most 33 Byzantine members and requires a quorum threshold of 67.
A committee of 1000 validators tolerates at most 333 faulty members with a quorum threshold of 667.
With a 100-validator committee at pipeline depth 4, Solstice sustains 8900 transactions per second at 0.9 seconds median latency.
With a 4000-validator committee at pipeline depth 4, Solstice sustains 4200 transactions per second at 2.3 seconds median latency.
Two quorums each exceeding 2n/3 of committee stake intersect in more than n/3 of stake.

supporting_evidence.quote: |Q1 (intersect) Q2| > 2n/3 + 2n/3 - n = n/3

claim: With leaders chosen uniformly at random, the probability a chosen leader is faulty is bounded above by f/n.

supporting_evidence.quote: With f Byzantine validators out of n total, and leaders chosen uniformly at random, p is bounded above by f/n regardless of which specific validators are faulty.

claim: Solstice 1.1, released in February 2019, added the sentry-node relay architecture.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice 1.1, released in February 2019, added the sentry-node relay architecture described in the operations guide.

claim: Solstice 2.3, published in May 2021, raised the maximum supported committee size from 1,000 to 4,000 validators.

supporting_evidence.quote: Solstice 2.3, published in May 2021, raised the maximum supported committee size from 1,000 to 4,000 validators.

claim: The August 2023 partition was traced to a submarine cable fault that isolated eleven Southeast Asian validators for about six hours.

supporting_evidence.quote: The partition was traced to a submarine cable fault that isolated eleven validators in the Southeast Asian region for approximately six hours.

claim: A competing protocol sustained only 2,100 transactions per second on the MCB v2 wide-area profile, attributed to its quadratic signature-verification cost.

supporting_evidence.quote: A competing protocol evaluated on the same MCB v2 profile sustained only 2,100 transactions per second, which the benchmark authors attributed to its quadratic signature-verification cost.

claim: Running Solstice over the buoy array's satellite links produced median block times above eleven seconds.

supporting_evidence.quote: Running Solstice's committee protocol over that link budget produced median block times above eleven seconds, far outside the range seen in terrestrial deployments.

claim: No safety violation occurred during the partition; affected validators simply stopped participating in view-change quorums until connectivity returned.

supporting_evidence.quote: No safety violation occurred during the partition; the affected validators simply stopped participating in view-change quorums until connectivity was restored.

claim: Contemporary summaries describe the Neptune-9 production array as a 300-buoy deployment.

supporting_evidence.quote: Contemporary summaries of the experiment describe the full production array as a 300-buoy deployment spread across its designated monitoring region.

claim: Onboarding requires generating the validator signing key inside an approved hardware security module.

supporting_evidence.quote: 1. Generate a validator signing key inside an approved hardware security module.

claim: Clock drift beyond the tolerated skew causes a validator to time out and trigger unnecessary view changes.

supporting_evidence.quote: 1. Clock drift beyond the tolerated skew, which causes a validator to time out and trigger unnecessary view changes.

claim: Papaioannou's Greek-language study of safety, liveness, and efficiency unifies the Japanese and Russian leader-rotation results in a single framework.

supporting_evidence.quote: Papaioannou's study of ασφάλεια, ζωντάνια και αποδοτικότητα (safety, liveness, and efficiency) situates both results within a single unified framework still cited in later surveys.

claim: The German-language report Schwellenwert-Signaturen in verteilten Systemen (2020) independently validates the same threshold-signature parameter choices under a stricter adversarial model.

supporting_evidence.quote: A companion German-language report, Schwellenwert-Signaturen in verteilten Systemen (2020), independently validates the same parameter choices under a stricter adversarial model.
